tasks.prompt: Warm-up: Write the ConfigMap manifest. Create a file named `configmap.yaml` that defines a ConfigMap named `flask-config` with the label `app: flask-capstone` and two data entries: `APP_ENV` set to `"production"` and `APP_GREETING` set to `"Hello from Kubernetes!"`.
tasks.starter:
# configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: ___
  labels:
    app: ___
data:
  APP_ENV: ___
  APP_GREETING: ___
hints: The `kind` for a ConfigMap is `ConfigMap` and its `apiVersion` is `v1`.
The `metadata.name` must match the name you will reference in the Deployment's `configMapRef.name` field later — use `flask-config`.
Under `data`, each entry is a plain key: "value" pair with no special sub-keys.
tasks.solution:
# configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: flask-config
  labels:
    app: flask-capstone
data:
  APP_ENV: "production"
  APP_GREETING: "Hello from Kubernetes!"
tasks.explanation: A ConfigMap stores configuration outside the container image. At runtime, the Deployment injects every key in `data` as an environment variable into the container via `envFrom`. This means you can change `APP_ENV` from `production` to `staging` by editing only this file and restarting the Pods — the image itself never needs to be rebuilt.

tasks.prompt:
Core task 1: Write the Deployment manifest. Create `deployment.yaml` for the `flask-capstone` app with all of the following requirements:
- `replicas: 2`
- Container image `flask-capstone:1.0` with `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent`
- `containerPort: 5000`
- All keys from the `flask-config` ConfigMap injected as environment variables via `envFrom`
- Labels and selectors: both `spec.selector.matchLabels` and `spec.template.metadata.labels` must use `app: flask-capstone`
- A readiness probe: HTTP GET `/healthz` on port 5000, `initialDelaySeconds: 3`, `periodSeconds: 5`
- A liveness probe: HTTP GET `/healthz` on port 5000, `initialDelaySeconds: 10`, `periodSeconds: 10`
tasks.starter:
# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: flask-capstone
  labels:
    app: flask-capstone
spec:
  replicas: ___
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ___
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ___
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: flask
          image: ___
          imagePullPolicy: ___
          ports:
            - containerPort: ___
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: ___
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: ___
              port: ___
            initialDelaySeconds: ___
            periodSeconds: ___
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: ___
              port: ___
            initialDelaySeconds: ___
            periodSeconds: ___
hints: `spec.selector.matchLabels` and `spec.template.metadata.labels` must carry the identical label (`app: flask-capstone`). A mismatch causes the API server to reject the manifest.
Set `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` so Kubernetes uses the image already loaded into the cluster (from Lecture 3) instead of attempting a registry pull, which would fail with `ImagePullBackOff`.

Both the readiness probe and the liveness probe use `httpGet` with `path: /healthz` and `port: 5000`. The difference is in the timing: readiness uses `initialDelaySeconds: 3` / `periodSeconds: 5`, while liveness uses `initialDelaySeconds: 10` / `periodSeconds: 10`.
tasks.solution:
# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: flask-capstone
  labels:
    app: flask-capstone
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: flask-capstone
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: flask-capstone
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: flask
          image: flask-capstone:1.0
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: flask-config
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 5000
            initialDelaySeconds: 3
            periodSeconds: 5
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 5000
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 10
tasks.explanation: Three label blocks appear in the Deployment, each with a distinct role: `metadata.labels` tags the Deployment object itself; `spec.selector.matchLabels` is the rule the Deployment uses to identify which Pods it owns; `spec.template.metadata.labels` are the labels stamped onto every new Pod. The API server enforces that `matchLabels` equals the template labels — if they differ, the Deployment cannot recognise its own Pods and the manifest is rejected. `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` is mandatory when the image was side-loaded into the cluster, because there is no registry for Kubernetes to pull from. The readiness probe fires sooner and more frequently to remove unready Pods from Service traffic quickly; the liveness probe waits longer before the first check so the app has time to boot before a restart is ever considered.

tasks.prompt:
Core task 2: Write the Service manifest. Create `service.yaml` that exposes the `flask-capstone` Pods with the following properties:
- Type `ClusterIP`
- `selector: app: flask-capstone` (must match the Pod labels set by the Deployment)
- Service port `80` forwarding to container port `5000` over TCP
tasks.starter:
# service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: flask-capstone
  labels:
    app: flask-capstone
spec:
  type: ___
  selector:
    app: ___
  ports:
    - port: ___
      targetPort: ___
      protocol: ___
hints: `type: ClusterIP` makes the Service reachable only from inside the cluster. Other Pods in the cluster can reach the app at `flask-capstone:80`.
`port` is what callers use (80); `targetPort` is where the Service forwards the traffic on the container (5000). They serve different audiences and do not have to be the same number.
The Service's `selector` must carry `app: flask-capstone` — the same label the Deployment stamps onto each Pod — so the Service knows which Pods to include in its endpoint pool.
tasks.solution:
# service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: flask-capstone
  labels:
    app: flask-capstone
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: flask-capstone
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 5000
      protocol: TCP
tasks.explanation: A ClusterIP Service gives the group of Pods one stable internal IP and DNS name (`flask-capstone`) so callers never need to track individual Pod IPs, which change on every restart. The `selector: app: flask-capstone` is the same label the Deployment stamped onto each Pod; this is how the Service discovers its members — by label agreement, not by name reference. The `port: 80` / `targetPort: 5000` split lets the rest of the cluster communicate on the conventional HTTP port 80 while the Flask process continues to listen on 5000 inside the container. Only Pods that pass the readiness probe are added to the endpoint pool, which is why the readiness probe in the Deployment directly affects whether traffic reaches a given Pod.

tasks.prompt:
Application: A teammate hands you the following broken Deployment snippet. Identify and fix all three bugs — one label mismatch, one wrong imagePullPolicy, and one probe targeting the wrong port — then briefly explain what runtime failure each bug would have caused.

```yaml
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: flask-app          # BUG A
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: flask-capstone
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: flask
          image: flask-capstone:1.0
          imagePullPolicy: Always   # BUG B
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5000
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8080            # BUG C
            initialDelaySeconds: 3
            periodSeconds: 5
```
tasks.starter:
# Fix the three bugs and write the corrected snippet below.
# Then add a short comment after each corrected line explaining the failure the bug would have caused.

spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ___          # was: flask-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: flask-capstone
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: flask
          image: flask-capstone:1.0
          imagePullPolicy: ___  # was: Always
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5000
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: ___         # was: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 3
            periodSeconds: 5
hints: Bug A: compare `spec.selector.matchLabels.app` with `spec.template.metadata.labels.app`. The API server rejects any Deployment where these two values differ.
Bug B: the image `flask-capstone:1.0` was loaded directly into the cluster in Lecture 3 — it does not exist on any external registry. What happens when Kubernetes tries to pull it with `Always`?
Bug C: Flask listens on port 5000 inside the container, but the readiness probe is knocking on port 8080. What does Kubernetes do when the probe connection is refused?
tasks.solution:
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: flask-capstone   # FIXED A: was flask-app — must equal spec.template.metadata.labels.app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: flask-capstone
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: flask
          image: flask-capstone:1.0
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent  # FIXED B: was Always — Always forces a registry pull; no registry has this image, so Pods would stay in ImagePullBackOff
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5000
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 5000              # FIXED C: was 8080 — nothing listens on 8080, so the probe would always fail and the Pod would never enter the Service endpoint pool
            initialDelaySeconds: 3
            periodSeconds: 5
tasks.explanation: Bug A (label mismatch): Kubernetes requires `spec.selector.matchLabels` to equal `spec.template.metadata.labels`. The API server enforces this at admission time and rejects the manifest outright with a validation error — no Pods are ever created. Bug B (imagePullPolicy: Always): with `Always`, the kubelet ignores any locally cached image and always reaches out to a registry. Because `flask-capstone:1.0` was loaded directly into the cluster node (not pushed to Docker Hub or any registry), the pull fails and every Pod stays indefinitely in `ImagePullBackOff`. `IfNotPresent` tells the kubelet to use the image already present on the node and only pull if it is genuinely absent. Bug C (wrong probe port): the readiness probe attempts an HTTP GET on port 8080, but Flask listens on 5000. The connection is refused, the probe reports failure, and Kubernetes never marks the Pod as Ready — meaning the Service never adds the Pod to its endpoint pool and no traffic is ever routed to it, even though the app is actually running fine.
